data.title: Tesco: Guidance Gives Flexibility, Firepower to Respond to Current Market Conditions
Tesco Sees FY26 Retail FCF Within Midterm Guidance GBP1.4B-GBP1.8B
European airlines not pricing in 'worst case' scenarios, JPM says
data.description:
** J.P.Morgan says European airlines are not pricing in a full-blown global financial crisis scenario and cuts Air France-KLM to "neutral" from "overweight" seeing an outsized impact on the company's profitability in a downturn

** "AF-KLM remains financially and operationally geared to any small changes in demand in its markets," it says, highlighting its cash generation issues

** It says the sector remains volatile due to price declines and potential changes in passenger demand, after arrivals into the US from Western Europe dropped 17% in March

** The broker says Air France-KLM and Wizz Air would be pushed into losses in a severe downturn

** JPM says losses or consensus downgrades of about 60% could hit the sector in case of a full-blown global financial crisis, but adds 2025 might be milder than 2008-09

** It highlights "overweight"-rated Ryanair as a relative "safe haven" business model, and "underweight"-rated Lufthansa on the other hand, given Transatlantic risks and high costs

data.title: Poland - Factors to Watch April 10
data.description:
The following are significant news stories, press reports and events on Thursday. ALL TIMES GMT (Poland: GMT + 2 hour):

INTEREST RATES

Inflation in Poland is still high and in light of the March and prior inflation projections, any interest rate cut today is harmful to the Polish economy, central banker Joanna Tyrowicz told Dziennik Gazeta Prawna daily in an interview.

U.S. TARIFFS

The suspention of U.S. tariffs is "good news", it shows the U.S. administration's will to deescalate the trade conflict, Polish Finance Minister Andrzej Domanski said on Thursday.

CYFROWY POLSAT

Polish telecom and media firm Cyfrowy Polsat reported a 15.3% rise in quarterly adjusted core profit on Thursday, driven by stronger contributions from its green energy segment, higher retail revenue, and gains from apartment sales.

DEBT

On Thursday at 1000, state-owned Bank Gospodarstwa Krajowego will hold an bond tender.

****Reuters has not verified stories reported by Polish media and does not vouch for their accuracy.****

For other related news, double click on:

Polish equities E.Europe equities

data.title: Norway Inflation Rate Falls to 2.6% in March
data.description:
The annual consumer inflation rate in Norway eased to 2.6% in March 2025 from a ten-month high of 3.6% in February, falling below expectations of 2.9%.

The slowdown was largely driven by a sharp deceleration in housing and utilities inflation (1.3% vs 5.1%).

Price growth also moderated for furnishings, household equipment and maintenance (0.4% vs 1.8%), health (4.8% vs 5%), transport (1.5% vs 1.7%), and restaurants and hotels (3.4% vs 3.9%).

On a monthly basis, consumer prices dropped 0.7%, reversing February’s 1.4% rise and exceeding the forecast 0.5% decline.

Meanwhile, the CPI adjusted for tax changes and excluding energy products (CPI-ATE) rose 3.4% year-on-year, matching February’s rate and forecasts.

On a monthly basis, it increased 0.2%, down from a 1% rise in February, but slightly above the expected 0.1% gain.

data.title: Amcor Completes Construction of Malaysia Coating Facility for Healthcare Packaging, Shares Rise 4%
data.description:
Amcor completed construction of its advanced coating facility for healthcare packaging in Malaysia, according to a Thursday release.

The facility expands the firm's existing healthcare packaging plant in Selangor and is equipped with advanced technologies such as water-based coating systems, online inspection systems, and air knife technology, the release said.

The facility is part of its commitment to expanding its healthcare capabilities in the Asia Pacific region. It is the first in Asia to produce both top and bottom substrates for medical device packaging, per the release.

Its shares rose over 4% in recent trading on Thursday.

data.title: Romania Q4 GDP Growth Revised Downward
data.description:
Romania's GDP advanced 0.5% year-on-year in the fourth quarter of 2024, softer than the second estimate of 0.7%, easing from an upwardly revised 1.5% in the previous three-month period.

This marked the softest growth since the first quarter, amid sharp declines in fixed investments (-17.9%) and a negative contribution from net external demand, with exports down 3.0% and imports up 5.3%.

The decline was partially offset by a 6.7% rise in household consumption and a 4.3% increase in government spending.

On a seasonally adjusted quarterly basis, the economy expanded 0.6% in Q4, rebounding from a 0.1% contraction in the previous period.

For 2024 as a whole, GDP grew 0.8%, slowing from 2.4% in 2023

data.title: Corporate credit tremors in aftershock of tariff-led stock rout
data.description:
Key points:
  • Corporate credit under scrutiny as recession odds shorten
  • Investors demanding wider spreads to cover rising default risk
  • Few signs of panic so far but outflows, selloff seen likely

The tariff shock and recession fears that have sent world stocks into a tailspin over the last week are rolling into corporate funding markets, raising the cost of borrowing and disrupting financing plans even for lower-risk companies.

With U.S. Treasuries nursing huge losses on Wednesday - the strongest sign yet that stress is impacting so-called safe-haven assets - attention has now turned to the $35 trillion global corporate bond market, which has swelled by around 40% since 2008 as companies gorged on cheap debt, OECD data shows.

The premium investors demand to hold low-rated corporate credit (.MERHW00) versus government debt has soared by 100 basis points in a week, the biggest short-term move in so-called global junk bond spreads since the U.S. regional banking crisis in March 2023.

The move is fuelling fears pension funds and other longer-term investors might also start purging higher-quality borrowers from their portfolios. With the majority of fixed-income trading happening off-market, it can be hard to track and measure sales.

But the sharp dip in sentiment is far easier to discern. An index measuring the cost of insuring against debt defaults by Europe's strongest businesses hit its highest since late 2023 (ITEEU5Y=MP) on Wednesday.

German energy group RWE, which has an investment grade rating, was among companies that have been affected by the turmoil. Even though it hired banks to issue a green bond last month, it was unable to launch the sale amid the tariff news and ensuing market volatility, two people familiar with the matter said. RWE declined to comment.

In Japan, three companies have postponed the sale of 100 billion yen ($680 million) worth of yen-denominated bonds this week.

For some investors, it's a question of when contagion grips, rather than if.

"What you can clearly observe is that liquidity on the credit side has dried up," Lazard Asset Management global fixed income co-head Michael Weidner said.

"Liquidity and trading activity grinds to a halt first in loan and high-yield markets and then moves over to IG (investment grade) credit."

NO INVESTMENT GRADE CRISIS, YET

For now, most investors and analysts are predicting greater ructions in the high-yield market, which is populated by companies with heavy debt burdens, patchy earnings profiles and prospects broadly viewed as most at risk from recession.

Aberdeen investment grade credit director Luke Hickmore said he saw few signs of panic among investors in lower-risk debt securities, while credit strategists at UBS have told clients the current situation in credit markets was "nowhere close to the absolute worst case."

Chris Arcari, head of capital markets at financial services and pension fund consultant Hymans Robertson, was so far sanguine about the moves.

"We're coming from a position where spreads were very, very tight following a lot of yield-driven demand," he told Reuters.

"I don't see any huge contagion. I think there's a rational repricing if that makes sense. It's not disaster stations."

Investors pumped record sums into U.S. dollar-denominated investment grade debt funds last year, according to data from LSEG Lipper. For euro-denominated funds, inflows were the biggest since 2019.

A phase of net outflows from those funds - and a selloff in some of the names most sensitive to a trade war - was increasingly likely, analysts said, particularly after the first post-tariff data is published.

Edmond de Rothschild Asset Management head of multi-asset Michael Nizard, who said he started backing out of high-yield debt in late March, added that money managers were also likely to be selling investment grade debt to shore up cash reserves.

"They face outflows from clients and so they need to sell for cash," he said, predicting a "new round" of credit spreads widening in the short term.

But investment-grade defaults were rare even during the pandemic, which outside the two world wars, represented the greatest "economic full stop in modern times", Aberdeen's Hickmore said.

Citi strategists noted on Tuesday that the darkening credit outlook had pushed pricing of three recently issued high-yield bonds beyond 10 percentage points over the risk-free rate.

In a pessimistic scenario where high-yield spreads continue to widen sharply, Sharon Ou, Vice President and Senior Credit Officer at Moody's Ratings, said the global default rate could exceed 8% in a year's time from less than 5% today.

"The market dynamics have changed in the sense that this isn't purely about who's exposed to tariffs or not, this is a big negative shock for the overall economy, and that affects everyone," said Viktor Hjort, head of credit research at BNP Paribas .

"But have we seen a panic event? Nothing close to what we saw in 2022."

'FED PUT?'

Investors said only a rollback of tariffs by the White House or central bank rate cuts could insulate credit markets from here.

RW Baird strategist Ross Mayfield said he still favoured high-quality companies' debt, but risks for investment grade were increasing, with even a U-turn by Trump on tariffs potentially adding to market and business uncertainty instead of rebuilding confidence.

And even if the Fed does cut rates to soothe roiled markets, relief for companies could be short-lived.

According to the OECD, at the end of 2024, 63% of investment grade debt and 74% of non-investment grade debt had interest costs below the prevailing market rates and most will likely require refinancing at a higher cost.

"In a stagflationary environment from tariffs, you'll see both investment grade and high yield corporate borrowers struggle as their costs of debt rise," Mayfield said.

data.title: NBCC (India) Sells Over 1,100 Units in Greater Noida Project for INR15 Billion
data.description:
NBCC (India) sold 1,185 residential units at its Aspire Dream Valley Phase-III project in Greater Noida (West), Uttar Pradesh, India, according to a Wednesday filing.

The units were sold through an e-auction. The total sale value stood at approximately 15 billion Indian rupees, the filing said.

data.description:
Britain's Financial Reporting Council said on Thursday it had fined Ernst & Young 6.5 million pounds ($8.4 million) over audits of Thomas Cook for the financial years ended 2017 and 2018.

data.title: Energy trader Danske Commodities posts 48% fall in 2024 earnings
data.description:
Danish energy trader Danske Commodities on Thursday reported a 48% drop in profits for 2024, citing tough market conditions, especially for natural gas, impacted by what it called political interference and geopolitical risk.

Danske Commodities, owned by Norwegian oil and gas producer Equinor , posted full-year adjusted earnings before tax of 186 million euros, down from 359 million euros in 2023.

This was at the lower end of its guided range of 150 million and 350 million euros given by the company a year ago.

"Our gas business struggled due to non-fundamentally driven market conditions while we saw a strong performance from our power business," CEO Helle Oestergaard Kristiansen said in a statement.

data.title: Market Participants Beg for 'Just One Normal Day'Market Talk
data.description: After another highly volatile trading day in equities and U.S. Treasurys on Wednesday, market participants wish just a bit of calm, says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. "Just one normal and calm-ish day. Please," the senior research strategist says in a note. "It's all that I, and other market participants are asking for right now," he says. U.S. President Trump's sweeping tariffs went into effect Wednesday but later in the day, Trump announced a 90-day pause on certain tariffs to most countries. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0611 GMT - After another highly volatile trading day in equities and U.S. Treasurys on Wednesday, market participants wish just a bit of calm, says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. "Just one normal and calm-ish day. Please," the senior research strategist says in a note. "It's all that I, and other market participants are asking for right now," he says. U.S. President Trump's sweeping tariffs went into effect Wednesday but later in the day, Trump announced a 90-day pause on certain tariffs to most countries. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0607 GMT - U.S. CPI data may divert some attention away from the tariffs saga, says Commerzbank Research's Hauke Siemssen in a note. "Falling headline inflation may provide relief as energy prices declined, while the month-on-month core rate looks set to rise stronger and markets are likely to remain wary about the looming upward pull of tariffs on U.S. prices," the rates strategist says. Yet, a benign inflation print could slow weakness in U.S. Treasurys, he says. The headline month-on-month CPI is expected to have risen 0.1% in March, slowing from 0.2% in February, according to a poll of analysts by The Wall Street Journal. The month-on-month core CPI is expected at 0.2%, the same as in the previous month. The U.S. CPI data are due at 1230 GMT. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0604 GMT - A pause to President Trump's steep trade tariffs should be greeted with caution in Europe, ING economist Carsten Brzeski says. The president on Wednesday said he would roll back a major part of his tariff plan amid market turmoil. That grants a reprieve to European exporters, Brzeski says, with the EU's own countermeasures against some American products now unlikely to be put into effect, at least not immediately. But the harm to confidence will last, he says."In all honesty, the current situation is not only chaotic, it's crazy," Brsezki says.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0600 GMT - The U.S. Treasury's $22 billion auction of 30-year bonds will be watched closely after highly volatile trading in U.S. debt this week. In Wednesday's trade, 30-year Treasury yields stand out, says Commerzbank Research's Hauke Siemssen. The 30-year Treasury yield hit 5% in Asian trade before consolidating. "The selling pressure might have been compounded by hedge funds unwinding basis trades, i.e. long cash bond/short futures position," the rates strategist says. The 30-year Treasury yield last trades 9.5 basis points lower at 4.696%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0545 GMT - Goldman Sachs analysts revert to their previous non-recession baseline forecast for the U.S. GDP growth, expectating it at 0.5% with a 45% probability of a recession, they say in a note. U.S. President Trump has announced a 90-day pause on certain tariffs to most countries. Before Trump's announcement, Goldman Sachs analysts had shifted to a recession baseline in response to the additional country-specific tariffs that went into effect Wednesday morning. As a result of the pause, "we are now reverting to our previous non-recession baseline forecast, which projected 2025 Q4/Q4 GDP growth of 0.5%, a peak core PCE inflation rate of 3.5%, a 45% probability of recession, and three consecutive 25bp 'insurance' cuts by the Federal Reserve in June, July, and September," the analysts say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0535 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields drop in the early European trading hours, reversing from Wednesday's double-digit increases. The rise in yields was prompted by news that U.S. President Trump announced a 90-day pause on certain tariffs for most countries, even as he raised tariffs on China to 125%. "Despite the calming winds, implied volatility in fixed income markets will likely remain elevated as the administration's next move remains very unpredictable," Danske Bank Research's Frederik Romedahl says in a note. The two-year Treasury yield is 5.5 basis points lower at 3.893%; the 10-year Treasury yield is down 11 basis points at 4.287% and the 30-year Treasury yield is also 10 basis points lower at 4.693%, according to Tradeweb data. Trading volumes in these hours are likely thinner. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0528 GMT - Higher U.S. tariffs on China will likely weigh significantly on the Chinese economy and labor market, Goldman Sachs economists say. "We are revising our real GDP growth forecasts for 2025 and 2026 downward to 4.0% and 3.5%, respectively, from our previous projections of 4.5% and 4.0%," they say. President Trump's additional tariff hikes are likely to have a diminishing marginal impact, but the steep increase is still set to weigh on the economy. GS expects Beijing to intensify policy easing, and now sees 60 bps of policy rate cuts versus 40 bps previously. It also anticipates a 4.1 ppt expansion in its estimate of the "augmented fiscal deficit" this year to 14.5% of its GDP. "However, even these significant easing measures are unlikely to fully offset the negative effects of the tariffs," they say. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0516 GMT - With a high degree of uncertainty surrounding U.S. trade policy likely to persist through early July, the Bank of Japan is expected to hold interest rates steady at its next meeting on April 30 and May 1, Morgan Stanley MUFG Securities economists say. "The BOJ will need to assess the negative impact of the 25% autos tariff on SME [small and medium enterprise} wage negotiations taking place over April-June," they say in a research note. The economists see the next rate increase as most likely in September, with some probability of a rate rise in July, depending on forex moves and the upper house election results. They expect the BOJ to pause monetary tightening after raising the policy rate to 0.75% from the current 0.5%. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0503 GMT - Bear flattening pressure persists in U.S. Treasurys, as markets trim Fed rate-cut expectations, analysts say. The latest surge in the two-year yield comes amid paring of Fed rate-cut bets, DBS Group Research's Eugene Leow says in commentary. "Meanwhile, the rapid selloff in long end U.S. (Treasurys) also cooled as market dysfunction eased somewhat," the senior rates strategist says. A bear flattening typically refers to short-term yields rising faster than long-dated yields on expectations of tighter monetary policy. Two-year Treasury yield is up 3bps at 3.8932%, while 10-year yield is down 1bp at 4.2855%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: REG - US Solar Fund PLC US Solar Fund - USFP - Dividend Declaration
data.description:
RNS Number : 3828E US Solar Fund PLC 10 April 2025  

10 April 2025

US SOLAR FUND PLC (the "Company")

Dividend Declaration

US Solar Fund plc (LON: USF (USD)/USFP (GBP)) is pleased to announce its Q4 2024 dividend of US$0.57 cents per Ordinary Share in line with its annual dividend target of US$2.25 cents per Ordinary Share. The dividend will be paid as timetabled below:

o 

Ex-Dividend Date: 17 April 2025

o 

Record Date: 22 April 2025

o 

Payment Date: 6 May 2025

Of this dividend declared of US$0.57 cents per Ordinary Share, US$0.34 cents per Ordinary Share has been designated as an interest distribution while the remaining US$0.23 cents per Ordinary Share will be paid as a dividend.

10th April 2025

Sound Energy PLC

("Sound" or the "Company")

Notification of Investor Presentation

Sound Energy PLC, (AIM:SOU) the AIM listed transition energy company,  is pleased to announce that following the announcement of the Company's Final Financial Results today Graham Lyon , the Company's Executive Chairman, Gary Dempster, Chief Financial Officer and John Argent, VP Geoscience will be holding a live Investor presentation and Q&A session via the Investor Meet Company platform on 23rd April 2025 at 5.30 pm (UK Local time).

data.title: REG - Foresight Group Hldg - Appointment of Joint Corporate Brokers
data.description:
RNS Number : 4086E Foresight Group Holdings Limited 10 April 2025  

LEI: 213800NNT42FFIZB1T09

10 April 2025

Appointment of Joint Corporate Brokers

Foresight Group Holdings Limited ("Foresight", the "Group") is a leading investment manager offering institutional and retail investors a diverse range of private and listed investment solutions in real assets located in the UK, Europe and Australia, and growth capital for SME businesses across the UK and Ireland.

The Group is pleased to announce the appointment of Joh. Berenberg, Gossler & Co KG, London ("Berenberg") as Joint Corporate Broker, alongside Jefferies International Limited, with immediate effect.

Contacts:

Foresight Group Investors

H-Advisors Maitland (Financial PR)

Liz Scorer / Ben McGrory

Sam Cartwright

+44 (0) 796 696 6956 / +44 (0) 744 382 1577

ir@foresightgroup.eu

+44 (0) 782 725 4561

Foresight@h-advisors.global

Jefferies (Joint Corporate Broker)

James Umbers / Taha Ahmed

+44 (0) 207 029 8000

Berenberg (Joint Corporate Broker)

James Felix / John Welch / Dan Gee-Summons

+44 (0) 203 753 7800

About Foresight Group Holdings Limited

Founded in 1984, Foresight is a leading investment manager in real assets and capital for growth, operating across the UK, Europe, and Australia.

With decades of experience, Foresight offers investors access to attractive investment opportunities at the forefront of change. Foresight actively builds and grows investment solutions to support the energy transition, decarbonise industry, enhance nature recovery and realise the economic potential of ambitious companies.

A constituent of the FTSE 250 index, Foresight's diversified investment strategies combine financial and operational skillsets to maximise asset value and provide attractive returns to its investors. Its wide range of private and public funds is complemented with a variety of investment solutions designed for the retail market.

Visit https://foresight.group for more information.

Follow us on LinkedIn for key updates.
